name: Robert "Bob" Jameson
chief_complaint: My wife says my face is drooping and I can't get my words out right. My left arm won't work like it should.
onset_description: Wife noticed sudden onset of left facial droop, slurred speech, and left arm weakness at approximately 9:15 AM while patient was eating breakfast. Patient was reportedly normal when he woke at 7:30 AM. Last known well time is approximately 9:00 AM. It is now 10:45 AM.

left_sided_weakness.description: My left arm feels heavy and clumsy, I dropped my coffee cup. I can barely lift it. My left leg feels a little weak too but not as bad.
left_sided_weakness.character: sudden onset, left arm much worse than left leg
left_sided_weakness.severity: left arm 2/5 strength, left leg 4/5 strength
left_sided_weakness.location: left upper and lower extremity, arm predominant

facial_droop.description: Wife: 'The left side of his face is sagging, he can't smile right.'
facial_droop.onset: sudden, noticed at same time as arm weakness
facial_droop.character: left-sided facial asymmetry, lower face predominant
aggravating_factors: attempting to smile or show teeth
associated_symptoms: drooling from left side of mouth

dysarthria.description: I know what I... want to shay... the wordsh come out all... mushed up
dysarthria.onset: sudden, same time as other symptoms
dysarthria.character: slurred speech, patient aware of deficit, comprehension intact

left_arm_numbness.description: It feels tingly and numb, like it fell asleep but it won't wake up

past_medical_history: Atrial fibrillation (diagnosed 2 years ago, not on anticoagulation - patient refused warfarin)
allergies: Warfarin (patient refused due to fear of bleeding - documented as 'patient preference', not true allergy)
Contrast dye (mild hives with prior CT - premedicated successfully)

social_history.smoking: Quit 3 years ago after CABG, 40 pack-year history
social_history.alcohol: 1-2 beers a few times per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife of 48 years in a two-story house. Wife is primary support.
social_history.exercise: Very sedentary, wife encourages walks but he rarely goes
social_history.diet: Enjoys meat and potatoes, red meat 4-5 times per week, low vegetable intake. Wife tries to cook healthy but he adds salt to everything.

conditions.father: Stroke at age 65, died at 72 from second stroke

personality: frustrated and frightened by his deficits but trying to remain composed, a proud man who hates being dependent, defers to his wife for history
communication_style: Speaks slowly with slurred speech but is cognitively intact. Gets frustrated when he can't articulate words clearly. His wife fills in details. He nods or gives thumbs up with right hand to confirm. Occasionally says 'damn it' when struggling with words. Pre-morbid personality was reportedly a quiet, no-nonsense type.
pain_description_style: Denies pain entirely. Focuses more on the functional deficits: 'I can't use my arm' rather than any discomfort. When asked about headache specifically, firmly denies it.

critical_findings: Acute onset left-sided hemiparesis with facial droop and dysarthria - classic stroke presentation
Last known well approximately 1.5 hours ago - within tPA window (< 4.5 hours)
Known atrial fibrillation WITHOUT anticoagulation - major cardioembolic stroke risk
Multiple stroke risk factors: AFib, HTN, DM2, hyperlipidemia, prior CVA/TIA, smoking history, obesity, family history
Hypertensive on presentation (178/96) - must manage carefully in acute stroke
History of contrast reaction - need premedication protocol for CTA
CHA2DS2-VASc score is high (age >75 with HTN, DM, vascular disease, likely >=5)
Prior carotid endarterectomy suggesting known cerebrovascular disease
Elevated glucose - must manage as hyperglycemia worsens stroke outcomes
rubric.diagnosis: Acute Ischemic Stroke (likely cardioembolic from atrial fibrillation)
differential_diagnoses: Bell's palsy (does not explain limb weakness)
recommended_interventions: STAT non-contrast CT head to rule out hemorrhage
CT angiography head and neck (with premedication for contrast allergy)
IV alteplase (tPA) if no contraindications and CT negative for hemorrhage
Consider mechanical thrombectomy if large vessel occlusion on CTA
Permissive hypertension up to 220/120 if no tPA; if tPA given, target <185/110
Dysphagia screening before any oral intake
Initiate anticoagulation discussion once acute phase managed (given AFib)
Admit to stroke unit or neuro ICU
